sentences.text: Yeah, there were many times, what we had.
But one-- some of them stays in your memory forever.
One of them is once we came, in the evening, from work.
And then we heard a command, [GERMAN], [GERMAN], faster, faster.
We were chased out of the block and taken to the railroad.
And there were a big load of gravel.
We didn't know what we have to do.
We were standing there, surprised.
And there weren't shovels.
There weren't-- not tools, nothing.
So the German said, what are you staying there and watching?
Begin to work.
We looked at them.
And they say, with your bare hand you have to take the gravel.
Where we'll put it?
They say, took off your jackets.
And put in your jacket.
And carry it to the camp.
So we did it.
And on the way, there was standing the guards, the German guards.
And they would whip us-- [? whip ?] us.
And who-- who had fell down or lose his load, the dogs would catch him and always kill them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Every time, we were running from the road to the camp.
One of-- one of the victims, the first one, was-- [INAUDIBLE].
He lost his load.
And the dogs was immediately at him.
And they teared him apart.
And German were laughing.
And we went on and on.
Every time the load became heavier and heavier for us.
I was praying, asking God, not to lose my load, or to have the strength to continue to run, and to carry the-- the gravel.
And it was endless.
I was thinking then, it will never end.
But after a few hours, you could see then it's not too much left over.
We lost a few people.
We were completely exhausted.
And we did the job.
So we went back to the barracks.
You-- you-- in some point, you are thinking only to survive this moment, to have strength to finish it, to-- to come through-- come through it.
You know what will-- what will happen to the man when he-- when he lost his load.
But it can happen to you the next minute.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once I had-- I had the honor to-- to get acquainted to Iron-- we called him the Iron [?
Gustav. ?] His real name is [?
Gustav ?] [INAUDIBLE].
He was an oberscharfuhrer.
He is-- he was a-- a criminal in his time.
He was a criminal, but a professional criminal, sitting in jail.
But in the time in German, he showed his-- his cruelty, his savage.
He was so savage that he-- they picked him up.
And when he got released from jail, they took him to a job in other camps.
S-- SS men.
But he couldn't get higher than oberscharfuhrer.
He wasn't an officer yet.
And he was mad about it.
He was so trying, hard-- hard trying.
And he couldn't get a higher rank.
So he-- his anger, he felt about it-- he let his anger go on us.
And he was-- sometime he in spillway commandant-- a commandant.
Then he was later sent to [SPEAKING GERMAN].
But a short time, he was in Kaiserwald.
And I had the luck to get acquainted to him.
So it was once, we went to work, the same where I was working for the organization-- organization, [INAUDIBLE].
And I got a small piece of bread.
I bought it, actually, from a-- from a Polish-- from a Polish man who-- who lived there already.
Some barracks was already-- they were already there.
And there was a quarrel between the guards.
And they decided to search this day, the Jewish-- oh, us.
And he found the piece of bread.
So he beat me up and took me to the camp, and let me stay on the fence.
The fence in Kaiserwald wasn't with electricity.
And I stood there maybe for an hour or more, waiting.
You know, I decide and that's all.
It's the end.
Usually, it was the end.
When something-- such things have happened, they could kill you on the spot, or take you inside, or send you you know, to Auschwitz, or other place.
And it was your end.
This time, they called me to the office.
And there was the Iron Gustav.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And another guard, also, a tyrant.
He was a Romanian.
And he's began to question me.
And the Iron-- the Iron Gustav was standing, questioning.
And the Romanian guard was flogging me.
He told me to bend down, to bend over.
And he was flogging me.
I don't know how many lashes I got.
But the Iron Gustav told him, you are not doing the right job.
I'll will show you how it has to be done.
And he showed him how you have to flog.
And he did it very good.
I felt it on my-- on my back, on my behind.
And every time, when I jerked my head, I couldn't stand on the place.
I jerked my head.
He would stand with his pistol in his hand, and blow me every time with the pistol, over my head.
So it continues.
I don't know, for me it was an eternity.
I don't know how long it went by.
And then he took me to the camp.
When I came in, I was dizzy.
And I thought it was-- that's all.
In the evening on the role call, they'll call my number.
And I'll get killed.
At this moment, all my life went by.
It was not a very long life.
I was 15 and a half years old.
But all my life, what was important for me, went through my head.
And my head was-- I had [? exe-curating ?] pain.
I have such a pain, excruciating pain.
My head was full with bruises.
My behind was as a fire.
And I lay down.
It wasn't allowed to lay down on your-- on your bunk bed doing the day till the night, when it was allowed.
But I didn't-- I said, what will be, will be.
I laid down, and I was thinking.
And in the evening, they didn't call my number.
And it went by so for months.
And they-- it was a time when they were playing games.
They will-- they wanted to show that they make some legal actions.
And they send the papers about me to Berlin.
And when I was in Buchenwald, one day they called me.
And I got the sentence was 20 lashes.
So I get it in Buchenwald, my sentence.
So it was another case, was what happened.
And every time, was it luck, or fate?
I don't know.
But I survived this time.
So twice you were punished for that piece of bread?
Yes.
When I bought the-- the bread, I would barter it for-- for something else.
Or-- not or-- but I would take a piece of bread to my uncle.
He was-- he didn't have now the power, the strength, to go to work.
And they took him to a special block.
They called it [SPEAKING GERMAN] block.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] block, it means a care, or a spare block.
And there were people who weren't able to go to work.
And I would take whatever I got, a piece of bread, a potato.
I would take it to my uncle.
And one day I came, and he wasn't there.
He was a big-- he was a strong man, and big.
But he became so-- so slim and so weak.
And one day they would-- they would make selections.
They would come and pick up the people who are weak, taking to kill them.
And one day I came, and my uncle, my dear uncle, was gone.
[SIGHS]
Yes.
They wanted to-- to look legal.
They organized-- they want to-- all the evidence, what they had.
In the forest of Rumbula, the forest of [INAUDIBLE], they want to destroy the evidence.
They want to burn the corpses.
And they would take, they called it [GERMAN].
It was a special commando they would take every day, when we would stay-- they would take from the jail-- from the main jail in Riga, they would take people, they would take from the camp.

So in the morning when we would go to work, there was a German guard would come, and say to our elders, the [GERMAN], so-called, the elder of the column, what we would go-- the group, we would go to work.
And he would say, I need two people from our small group.
So he would take two people, whatever, you know, from the last ones, the middles ones, the first ones.
And he took two people.
And the people were taken to the place, to Rumbula.
They made a-- a group sent to Rumbula.
And they were walking to-- to burn the corpse.
And then they killed the people, not to leave some witnesses.
They took people from Kaiserwald to Rumbula.
To Rumbula, to [INAUDIBLE] forest, so-called [GERMAN], they called it.
And they want to-- to destroy the evidence.
And-- but when we came, evidence anyways was there, you know, some bones leftover.
And-- the-- one-- even one grave leftover, mass grave.
But they tried to do it.
It was a time when they tried to destroy the evidence, and play a game as if everything was legal.
No, he wasn't in Kaiserwald.
No, he was all the time in the same [INAUDIBLE], in the same place.
They called it [GERMAN].
And there was better conditions than in-- in-- in Kaiserwald, or in other camps, because there were people who were specialists, who were working in their trade.
There were shoemakers, tailors, carpenters, and so on and so on.
So they were treated, eventually, better, you know, than other people in other camps.
And he tried, all the time, to-- to-- to get me there, but he couldn't.
So I didn't see him there.
You know, sometimes when people were sent to Kaiserwald, then I get a little card from him, but very rarely.
So--
The end of July, 1944, the last selection took place.
We were put-- we were undressed and running by a German officer who was standing there with his gloves-- leather gloves in his hand.
And he would make left, right, left, right.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
And we didn't know what it means to the left, what it means to the right.
But he selected.
And there on the right or on the left, one group would go to be killed.
The other group survived still.
So most of them who would-- who were taked to the left was people who were over-- over 30, or not more than 35, probably.
All the other-- what he-- it was how he wanted to.
But most of it, and only the young people survived.
And then we were taken on the 6th of August, 1944, by a vessel.
His name was, I think, [INAUDIBLE], is the name of it.
And we were taking-- taken to Danzig.
Now it's Gdansk.
And after the next day-- by-- about-- by the vessel, two nights and two days, and we arrived in Danzig.
And in the night, on barges, they took us to Stutthof Concentration Camp.
Stutthof, it was an extermination camp, Stutthof.

There were also there-- inside-- it's played a big role, you know, who was inside the-- the camp, who had the power inside the camp, because you have to spend some time, you know, inside the camp after work.
And as in Kaiserwald where we had the criminals, the professional criminals, the same thing was in Stutthof.
The criminals, German professional criminals, Poles, professional-- Pole-- Polish professional criminals, they had the power inside the camp.
There was also work.
All the time, you could see the smoke there, the-- gas-- gas chamber and crematorium.
And we were there for a short-- for a short time.
The conditions there were horrible.
The food, I was-- we were so hungry.
And sometimes you couldn't eat, the food so bitter.
It was a little soup.
I don't know what they put there.
And every time when you get to get the soup or the small piece of bread, He was beat you.
He would beat you.
So some of them got the piece of bread.
Some of-- some of us didn't.
And he would come, the kapo, the block elders, would come at night and beat us-- and beat us up.
We were lying two, three, four people in one-- in one bunk bed.
And he would say, it's not-- not in order.
You're-- you're-- you're [? stray ?] [? sick, ?] or whatever.
He would find a reason, and he would beat us up.
And then we-- but we weren't for a long time there.
On the first boat, we were two days and two nights.
With the barges from Danzig to Stutthof, took a night.
It was terrible.
We didn't have food.
We didn't have what to drink.
We didn't have toilets.
I got-- I got seasick.
I was dizzy lying there.
Because they put us on the bottom of the boat, you know, in the freight, cargoes, whatever it was with the-- It's for merchandise, not for people.
And they-- and they didn't allow us to go upstairs to get some air or to-- to get some water.
Only one or two people went there.
And so it was-- it was horrible.
And the same thing on the barges.
The barges were from fish.
And it was stinking.
And it was-- they closed the bottom.
They didn't have enough-- you know, they closed the top.
It was without air.
And such, we came there, you know, dizzy and sick.
It was-- it was even worse, even worse, because first of all, there was a-- the crematorium, the gas chamber was there.
And they could, you, send every minute, you know, to the gas chamber.
And, yes, if Kaiserwald was actually a labor concentration camp, Stutthof was an extermination camp with its own--
Yeah, we would see it coming, the-- the-- the-- the-- the smoke from the hole.
We weren't, for a long time, there.
And from-- from there, we went to-- they took us to Buchenwald.
And in beginning of September or end of August, we arrived in Buchenwald.
And there began another chapter of our prisoner's life.
Buchenwald was a different camp.
Buchenwald, the inside power was in the hand of the political prisoners.
So the inside condition were better, but not for all of the prisoners.
The Jewish, us, when we came in, when we arrived, they put us not in the barracks, but on the field.
There were a special-- a [? special ?] part of the camp who was, they called it quarantine.
So the people who arrived, us, they put it in the quarantine camp.
And there wasn't barracks, nothing.
We would sleep outside.
There was one small house-- a house or barrack, very small one without-- without windows, only two doors on both sides.
And they could-- We were about-- about 1,000 people, let's say.
And there could sleep only 100, if they were as sardines inside [INAUDIBLE].
So we would sleep outside.
It was very cold.
And daytime, they would take us to the quarry, where Austrian-Jewish worked.
And the guards around were Ukraine, from Ukraine, Ukrainians.
And there we would-- we would take a stone-- every one of us had to take a stone, go-- go down in the quarry, take a stone, come up, and then we make a group and go to the camp.
It was-- we worked as-- it came to your minds that we were are the same as the slaves from Egypt.
And so we would go from one side to the other.
The-- this gas chamber was working all the time.
You could feel the smell of bones and flesh.
And till now, many times I feel it, the smell.
And so was Buchenwald.
And--
Well-- they-- they were standing with their-- it was the case when Buchenwald was bombed by the Americans, daytime.
And the whole quarry there, the stones, began to flow inside the quarry, you know, from one side to the other.
And the people get killed.
They didn't allow them to leave the quarry.
And the people who were inside, about 50 people, were killed there from the stones.
Not from the bombs, but from the stones who would come from one side.
The bomb was close to the-- [? it was ?] [? only one-- ?] And they gotted killed from the stones.
So was the Ukrainian guards.
They were very cruel, very cruel.
And we-- we stood without-- stayed there without water.
And then after the bombing, and we were without water.
So and then again came the selections.
And there I witnessed what the Professor Mintz, what you asked me about the ghetto when he was a professor, famous Professor Mintz.
We were undressed, stripped naked.
And we would go-- there was a table standing there.
And there was sitting, German-- German guards, German officers.
And there was also, in a white robe, a Czech doctor.
And they would again, left and right.
And they didn't do it this time, left and right.
But they wrote down your number.
And the Professor Mintz came by.
And the Czech-- Czechish doctor, he knew him.
And he instinctively, you know, he jumped up and went to him, and give him his hand.
He greeted him.
And in this moment, the German officer went up, gave him a slap in his face, and took him away, the-- Czech-- Czechish doctor.
And Professor Mintz, he got sick there.
He got dysentery.
And they took him to the hospital.
And so he perished there.
It was the end of Professor Mintz.
In the camp, not in the ghetto.
In the concentration camp, in Buchenwald.
He was a prisoner.
I didn't know-- the doctor?
I didn't know the doctor.
Later, they told us that it was a Czechish doctor.
We didn't know who he is.
But we saw the officers, the German officers, and he was in a white robe.
But later, they told us.
Because the-- the inmates, they knew who he was.
And they told us that he's a Czechish doctor.
And he couldn't-- and you know, he couldn't refuse to-- to give it-- he-- he made it-- and he knew, probably, what will follow.
But he couldn't, you know, resist not to do it.
It-- they would bring some water.
And people would fight, you know, to get a drop.
We would go to the sink and so the water-- maybe a drop of water would come out.
And it was very hard, very hard without water.
But it's too-- later we were-- we were sent to another camp from Buchenwald.
It was after a month or so.
We were in Buchenwald.
And then they sent us-- they have the same [INAUDIBLE] that they had there, they had camps from the workplace where people work.
And we were sent to a [? synthetic ?] [INAUDIBLE] it's [NON-ENGLISH], it's called, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was in Ramsdorf.
They took us there.
And we were under the supervision of Buchenwald.
We were as Buchenwald prisoners, but working in there.
It was a tent camp.
They-- we were-- we were sleeping in the tent.
It was-- it was-- it was not a permanently camp.
They were building somewhere.
But till this time being, we were there.
And it was also horrible conditions there.
The elder of the camp was a gypsy, a criminal also.
And he was our-- our fate was in his hands.
And he-- he tried the best.
We were sent to the factory and working there.
I-- I-- I-- they asked what kind of profession you have.
And I said, I am a carpenter.
So they sent me to work there, you know, to do something, a carpenter job.
And there was-- it was cold.
We didn't have-- we didn't have-- we didn't have-- you know, the dress what they gave us in Buchenwald, it was a prisoner-- a prisoner, you know, the-- the uniform.
And they didn't look on your size.
They didn't look.
Oh no, whatever you had-- what they had in their hands, they throw it to you.
And I got one.
It was a short one, and then a very, very thin one.
You could see it through.
And it was very cold.
So we tried everything, you-- you know, to-- to keep-- to keep warm.
And we took some bags, you know, from paper bags, putting on under our-- making this vest under this.
But then we were searched.
And the same, the gypsy-- the-- the elder, the-- the camp elder, he punished us, and-- and-- and beat us, and took away everything.
So we were again, cold, you know, going to work.
And so was life there.
It was very hard life.
It was very, very cold. it was very cold.
But it was so crowded, and so many people in the tent, you know?
You had four tiers, the bunk beds.
And people were so crowded.
And you warmed up each other.
And so the morning again, in the-- it wasn't the frost.
It was about-- well, Celsius maybe, 13 or 14 degree.
But it was cold enough for your-- your being hungry, without food.
The food what they gave us, you know, it was not-- not to survive.
It was food, what it was, for a certain time to-- to exist.
And-- and the conditions, what we had, we were-- were-- were-- the-- the dress-- the-- the garments what we wear, it was-- I know we were very cold.
It was very cold.
The tents were big, yeah.
The tents were big.
And we were-- we were there for-- I don't know many months we were there.
And then we were taken to another camp.
They build another camp.
They build another camp.
But it was the same-- the condition was the same, only what it was not tents, more.
But they build some blocks again.
And it was the same gypsy-- we would stay for hours on the role calls.
People would fell.
And there will be-- if some people would be absent, you know, some would fell asleep in the-- in the barracks, didn't come to the role call, we would stand for hours till they would find the man.
They would count 10 times in frozen weather.
It was cases where we was there-- we were standing there for the whole night.
